confirmed.title: Heap buffer overflow building function signature: buffer size omits parameter type-annotation lengths
confirmed.trigger: Open/edit any .lat source via the LSP (textDocument/didOpen or didChange -> lsp_analyze_document -> extract_symbols) containing a function whose parameter has a long type annotation, e.g. `fn f(p: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA) {}`. The type-annotation identifier (fn->params[j].ty.name) is attacker-controlled and unbounded but is NOT counted in `siglen` (only the param NAME +16 is). Confirmed with AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow WRITE at lsp_analysis.c:124 into the 50-byte region allocated at line 114.
confirmed.consequence: Because snprintf returns the number of bytes it WOULD have written, `p += snprintf(...)` advances `p` past `end` on truncation, so on the next call `(size_t)(end - p)` underflows to a huge size_t and subsequent snprintf calls write the full, attacker-controlled type-name string past the heap allocation. With multiple long-typed params this is a large, content-controlled heap overflow -> memory corruption / potential RCE, triggered merely by opening a malicious source file in an editor using the Lattice LSP.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/critical] CONFIRMED real and reachable from untrusted input. The cited code in src/lsp_analysis.c:112-124 is exactly as described. siglen accounts for strlen(fn->name)+32 plus strlen(params[j].name)+16 per param, but the type-annotation string fn->params[j].ty.name (appended via ": %s" at line 122) is NEVER counted. Overflow occurs whenever a parameter's type-annotation identifier exceeds ~40 chars.

Reachability chain fully verified end-to-end:
1. clat-lsp is a real shipped binary: src/lsp_main.c:52 main() -> lsp_server_new() -> lsp_server_run() (Makefile LSP_TARGET=clat-lsp, LSP_SRCS includes lsp_main.c + lsp_analysis.c).
2. src/lsp_server.c:3471/3473 dispatch textDocument/didOpen and didChange. handle_did_open (line 513) and handle_did_change (line 531) read document text DIRECTLY from untrusted JSON (textDocument.text / contentChanges[last].text) with no length/content validation, then call lsp_analyze_document(doc) (lines 525, 556).
3. lsp_analyze_document (lsp_analysis.c:1012) lexes/parses the raw text and calls extract_symbols at line 1083 — and even at line 1080 on a PARTIAL AST when there is a parse error, widening reach.
4. extract_symbols ITEM_FUNCTION branch builds the signature with the buggy malloc.
5. Parser: parse_params sets params[n].ty = *te (parser.c:459) where te->name = expect_ident (parser.c:336). The lexer's read_ident (lexer.c:77-80) scans an UNBOUNDED run of isalnum/'_' — no identifier length cap exists anywhere.

Buffer math matches the claim precisely: for `fn f(p: <64 A's>)`, siglen = strlen("f")+32 + strlen("p")+16 = 50 (the exact 50-byte region from the ASan report). Output needed = "fn f(" (5) + "p" (1) + ": "+64 (66) + ")" (1) + NUL = 74 bytes > 50. At line 122, p += snprintf returns the would-be length (66), advancing p past end; at line 124 (size_t)(end - p) underflows to a huge size and snprintf writes ")\0" past the allocation — heap-buffer-overflow WRITE at line 124 exactly as reported. With multiple long-typed params, lines 121/122 of later params write the full attacker-controlled type/name strings past the buffer (large content-controlled overflow).

No upstream guard mitigates this: identifiers are unbounded, document text is untrusted, and extract_symbols runs unconditionally (including on syntax-error partial parses). Severity critical is justified: an attacker-controlled heap OOB write (controllable offset and content via multi-param escalation) triggered merely by opening a malicious .lat file in any editor using the Lattice LSP — a realistic memory-corruption / potential-RCE primitive. || [high/critical] Confirmed by reading the real code. src/lsp_analysis.c:112-113 computes siglen = strlen(fn->name)+32 plus, per param, strlen(params[j].name)+16 — the +16 covers only the ", " and ": " separators, NOT strlen(params[j].ty.name). The type-annotation length is omitted from the malloc at line 114. The type name is attacker-controlled and unbounded: Param.ty is a TypeExpr (include/ast.h:401, 50-54) whose .name is set from expect_ident() in parser.c:336 (parse_type_expr_inner), an arbitrary-length identifier from source text. The path is reachable from untrusted input: lsp_server.c:525/556 (didOpen/didChange) feed JSON-supplied document text into lsp_analyze_document -> extract_symbols (lsp_analysis.c:1080/1083 -> 98). The overflow mechanism is exactly as claimed: line 122 `p += snprintf(p, end-p, ": %s", ty.name)` truncates safely within the buffer but returns the would-be length (e.g. 66 for a 64-char type), advancing p past end; the subsequent `(size_t)(end - p)` underflows to ~2^64 and line 124's snprintf (and lines 120-122 for additional params) writes content-controlled bytes past the allocation. Worked example `fn f(p: <64 A's>)`: siglen=50, p reaches buf+72, line 124 writes ")"+NUL at offset 72 in a 50-byte heap buffer. This is a content-controlled heap buffer overflow reachable merely by opening a malicious .lat file in an editor using the Lattice LSP — memory corruption with RCE potential. Severity critical is appropriate. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or any existing check; no guard prevents the write. || [high/critical] Confirmed real heap-buffer-overflow in src/lsp_analysis.c:112-124. The signature buffer size is computed as siglen = strlen(fn->name) + 32 plus per-param strlen(params[j].name) + 16 (lines 112-113). The +16 per param only accounts for the ", " and ": " separators; strlen(fn->params[j].ty.name) — the type-annotation identifier emitted at line 122 via snprintf(p, ..., ": %s", ty.name) — is never added. ty.name (include/ast.h:400-401, TypeExpr.name at ast.h:53) is populated by read_ident (src/lexer.c:77-86), which grows identifiers dynamically with NO length cap (the 256 at lexer.c:732 is for triple-quoted strings, irrelevant). So ty.name is attacker-controlled and unbounded. For `fn f(p: <64 A's>)`, siglen = 1+32+(1+16) = 50 bytes (exactly the 50-byte ASan region claimed), but the rendered string needs 74 bytes. The overflow mechanism is precisely as described: p += snprintf(...) advances p by the would-have-written count (not truncated count), so once a write truncates, p passes end; the subsequent (size_t)(end - p) underflows to a huge value and snprintf then writes at p past the allocation. The final snprintf(p, (size_t)(end - p), ")") at line 124 is the OOB WRITE; with multiple long-typed params, line 122 writes the full attacker-controlled type string past the heap buffer, yielding a large content-controlled overflow. Reachability is confirmed from untrusted input: handle_did_open (src/lsp_server.c:525) and handle_did_change (src/lsp_server.c:556) both invoke lsp_analyze_document -> extract_symbols (lsp_analysis.c:1080/1083) on every didOpen/didChange. I searched for any guard (length cap, null check, bound) and found none. Severity critical is justified: a content-controlled heap write triggered merely by opening a malicious source file in any editor using the Lattice LSP.

confirmed.title: Heap buffer overflow building impl-method signature: same under-sized buffer pattern (type names uncounted)
confirmed.trigger: Open via the LSP an impl block whose method has a long parameter type annotation, e.g. `impl Foo for P { fn m(self: P, q: BBBالبB...80+ chars) {} }`. The same `p += snprintf` over-advance occurs because `msiglen` omits `fn->params[k].ty.name` lengths. Confirmed with AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow WRITE at lsp_analysis.c:308 into the region allocated at line 297.
confirmed.consequence: Identical mechanism to the function-signature overflow: `(size_t)(end - p)` underflows after `p` passes `end`, and subsequent snprintf writes attacker-controlled type-name bytes past the heap buffer. Reachable from untrusted source via textDocument/didOpen/didChange; heap corruption / potential RCE.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] CONFIRMED REAL and REACHABLE from untrusted input. The bug and its trigger path both check out.

Buffer math bug (src/lsp_analysis.c:295-308): The allocation only counts `strlen(fn->name)+32` plus `strlen(params[k].name)+16` per param. It never accounts for `fn->params[k].ty.name`, yet line 306 writes `": %s"` with that type name. The `p += snprintf(...)` idiom advances `p` by the *would-be* length (not bytes actually written), so once a long type name is emitted, `p` passes `end`; then `(size_t)(end - p)` wraps to a huge value and the next snprintf (line 303/304/306 of the following param, or `")"` at 308) writes past the heap allocation. This is structurally identical to the function-signature path at lines 112-124.

Reachability chain (fully verified, no guards):
1. Untrusted entry: src/lsp_server.c handle_did_open (line 513-525) and handle_did_change (531-556) read the `text` field of a `textDocument/didOpen`/`didChange` JSON-RPC message and call `lsp_analyze_document(doc)`. The LSP is a real, built binary (`clat-lsp` via `make lsp`, src/lsp_main.c main()).
2. lsp_analyze_document (src/lsp_analysis.c:1052-1095) lexes + parses the text and calls extract_symbols — including on a PARTIAL AST when parsing fails (line 1080), as well as on success (1083).
3. extract_symbols ITEM_IMPL branch (line 287) reaches the vulnerable loop whenever `ib->type_name && ib->method_count > 0`. parse_impl_block (src/parser.c:3023) requires `impl Trait for Type { fn ... }`, exactly the claimed trigger.
4. Type names are stored verbatim: parse_type_expr_inner sets `te->name = expect_ident(...)` (parser.c:336), and the lexer's read_ident (src/lexer.c:77-79) has NO length cap (`while isalnum||'_'`). So `params[k].ty.name` is an arbitrarily long, attacker-controlled string.

I built an ASAN replica of the exact lines 295-308. With a method `fn m(a: <80-char type>, evilparam: EvilType)` it aborts with: "heap-buffer-overflow ... WRITE of size 1 ... 29 bytes after 70-byte region", in the snprintf at the replica of line 306 — demonstrating that the subsequent param's name/type bytes (attacker-controlled) are written out of bounds at an attacker-controlled offset, matching the claim's described mechanism precisely.

Severity: I downgrade critical -> high. It is a genuine heap OOB WRITE with attacker-controlled content and offset (a meaningful corruption primitive, potential RCE), reachable simply by opening a malicious .lat file in an editor running clat-lsp (a realistic drive-by/supply-chain vector). However it is confined to the optional LSP tooling binary rather than the core interpreter/runtime that executes scripts, and the writes are NUL-terminated/snprintf-bounded per call, so "high" is the most calibrated rating; "critical" is defensible but the non-core attack surface argues for high. || [high/critical] The claimed heap-buffer-overflow is real and the mechanism holds exactly as described, verified against the actual code.

Buffer sizing (src/lsp_analysis.c:295-296): `size_t msiglen = strlen(fn->name) + 32;` then per-param `msiglen += strlen(fn->params[k].name) + 16;`. The per-param type-name length (`fn->params[k].ty.name`) is omitted entirely. The +16 per param only covers the separators ", " (2) and ": " (2) plus ~12 bytes slack — nothing for the type name itself.

The uncounted write occurs at line 306: `p += snprintf(p, (size_t)(end - p), ": %s", fn->params[k].ty.name);`. `Param.ty` is an embedded `TypeExpr` (include/ast.h:401), and `TypeExpr.name` is `char *name` holding the raw TYPE_NAMED identifier (include/ast.h:54). The lexer reads identifiers with an unbounded loop `while (isalnum || '_')` (src/lexer.c:79) — no length cap — so type names are attacker-controlled and arbitrarily long.

Over-advance/underflow: snprintf returns the would-be length, so `p += snprintf(...)` advances p past `end` once a long type name truncates. After that, `(size_t)(end - p)` underflows to a huge value, and the subsequent snprintf at line 308 (`")"`) — and, with a following parameter, lines 303/304/306 — writes at p which is now past the allocation, with an effectively unbounded size argument. Concrete trace for `fn m(self: P, q: <80-char type>)`: msiglen = 1+32 + (4+16) + (1+16) = 70; after writing "fn m(self: P, q: " the long-type snprintf returns 82 and advances p to offset ~97 (buffer is 70), then `snprintf(p, (size_t)(70-97)=huge, ")")` writes ")"+NUL ~27 bytes past the 70-byte heap block. With multiple long-typed params, the name/type writes after the overflow point get the huge size and write full attacker-controlled bytes out of bounds.

Reachability is confirmed: lsp_server.c didOpen (525) / didChange (556) -> lsp_analyze_document (lsp_analysis.c:1012) -> extract_symbols (98) -> ITEM_IMPL branch (266-318). Even on partial parse error, line 1080 still invokes extract_symbols on the partial AST, so partially-valid malicious source triggers it. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or any existing guard — there is no length check anywhere on the path. Severity critical is appropriate: untrusted-source-triggered heap overflow WRITE with attacker-controlled length and content. || [high/critical] CONFIRMED real critical heap OOB write in src/lsp_analysis.c:295-308 (ITEM_IMPL impl-method signature builder). I tried to refute it and could not find any guard.

Buffer sizing (lines 295-296): msiglen = strlen(fn->name) + 32, plus per-param strlen(params[k].name) + 16. It counts param NAMES but never counts fn->params[k].ty.name, even though line 306 writes ": %s" with the full type name. For a single typed param: allocated = strlen(fn->name)+strlen(name)+48, while written (would-be) = strlen(fn->name)+strlen(name)+strlen(ty)+8. Overflow occurs whenever strlen(ty) > 40; the claimed 80-char type name is well past that.

Mechanism is the classic p += snprintf antipattern: snprintf returns the would-be length, so line 306 advances p PAST end (snprintf itself truncates safely, but p still jumps). The next call (line 308 for the last/single param, or line 303 ", " for a non-last param) computes (size_t)(end - p) which is a negative ptrdiff wrapped to a huge size_t; snprintf is then told it has an enormous buffer and writes ")\0" at p, which is already beyond the malloc'd region. Worked example: fn name "m", param "q", 80-char type → msiglen=50, p ends at offset 88, write lands ~38 bytes past the 50-byte buffer. No code clamps p to end.

Unbounded attacker input: parser.c:336 sets te->name = expect_ident(...), a strdup of an arbitrary-length identifier token — no length cap. Param type annotations in impl methods are parsed via parse_params (parser.c:2630) which stores params[n].ty = *te.

Reachability from untrusted source: lsp_server.c handle_did_open (525) / handle_did_change (556) → lsp_analyze_document → extract_symbols → ITEM_IMPL (lines 287-318). Triggered even on partial parse (line 1080). Document text is fully attacker-controlled via LSP. This is the same already-known pattern as the top-level function signature builder at lines 112-123. Severity critical (heap corruption with attacker-controlled type-name bytes, reachable from untrusted source) is correct.

confirmed.title: 32-bit wraparound in Buffer.write_u16/u32/u64/i64 bounds check → controlled heap-underflow OOB write (wasm32)
confirmed.trigger: Untrusted Lattice source passed to the exported lat_run_line / lat_run_line_regvm: `flux b = Buffer::new(16); b.write_u32(4294967293, 305419896)`. On wasm32 size_t is 32-bit, so the index arg (a 64-bit Lattice Int) is truncated by `(size_t)args[0].as.int_val` and the `+4` overflows 32-bit: (size_t)0xFFFFFFFD + 4 wraps to 1, which is <= len=16, so the bounds check passes. The subsequent store index i=0xFFFFFFFD makes data[i] resolve (32-bit pointer wrap) to data-3, giving an attacker-chosen-byte write at a small negative offset just before the heap allocation. On 64-bit hosts the same code is safe (no truncation, the +N can't overflow, oversize index is rejected).
confirmed.consequence: Heap-buffer-overflow WRITE of attacker-controlled bytes at attacker-chosen small negative offset from any Buffer (verified with AddressSanitizer on the real emcc wasm build: `heap-buffer-overflow WRITE of size 1 ... 3 bytes before 16-byte region`, stack lat_run_line→stackvm_run→builtin_buffer_write_u32, and independently via lat_run_line_regvm). Corrupts adjacent allocations / allocator metadata inside the wasm linear memory — a precise memory-corruption primitive usable from any site that runs untrusted Lattice source through the WASM module (e.g. an online playground or server-side wasm sandbox).
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] CONFIRMED REACHABLE and exploitable on the wasm32 build. The cited code matches reality: builtin_methods.c:1243 (write_u32) checks `(size_t)args[0].as.int_val + 4 > obj->as.buffer.len`, then stores into `obj->as.buffer.data[i]`...`[i+3]` with `size_t i = (size_t)args[0].as.int_val`. Same pattern at lines 1214 (write_u16), 1333 (write_u64), 1368 (write_i64), and the RegVM inline paths regvm.c:2302 (write_u16) and 2331 (write_u32: `(size_t)idx + 3 < len`), with regvm.c:2422/2434 routing write_u64/i64 to the same builtins.

Reachability is solid with no upstream guard:
1. src/wasm_api.c exports lat_run_line (line 87) and lat_run_line_regvm (line 246) via EMSCRIPTEN_KEEPALIVE and -sEXPORTED_FUNCTIONS (Makefile:286). Both take raw untrusted `source` and lex→parse→compile→run with NO index/value validation.
2. The literal 4294967293 (0xFFFFFFFD) is parsed by strtoll (lexer.c:210) into a positive int64 with no clamping (only ERANGE for >2^63).
3. Buffer::new(16) (runtime.c:372-375) allocates a 16-byte buffer; flux makes it writable; no capability gate on Buffer methods.
4. StackVM dispatch (stackvm.c:2478-2486) pops the 2 args and passes them UNCHANGED to builtin_buffer_write_u32(obj, args, 2). RegVM has equivalent inline/builtin paths.
5. builtin_methods.c, stackvm.c, regvm.c are all in WASM_SRCS (not in WASM_EXCLUDE, Makefile:280-283).

The truncation/overflow is genuine on wasm32 (32-bit size_t): the `< 0` guard passes (value positive as int64); `(size_t)0xFFFFFFFD + 4` wraps to 1, which is <= len=16, so the bounds check passes and no error is set; then data[0xFFFFFFFD] resolves via 32-bit pointer wrap to data-3, yielding a 4-byte attacker-controlled OOB write at an attacker-chosen small negative offset before the allocation. The finding correctly scopes this to wasm32 — on 64-bit hosts size_t is 64-bit, no truncation, idx+N can't overflow, and oversize indices are properly rejected, which is why the native test suite never trips it.

This is the precise untrusted-input surface the WASM module exists for (online playground / server-side wasm sandbox running arbitrary user Lattice). A controlled OOB write of attacker-chosen bytes at an attacker-chosen offset, reachable via a trivial one-liner, corrupting adjacent allocations / allocator metadata inside the wasm linear memory and able to defeat Lattice-level safety/isolation in a multi-tenant sandbox. Severity high is appropriate (confined to the wasm sandbox and to the wasm32 platform, but it is a clean, controlled memory-corruption primitive on exactly the threat-model surface). || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from actual code and fully confirmed. (1) The index arg is int64_t (value.h:56 `int_val`); buffer.len is size_t and buffer.data is uint8_t* (value.h:111-113). (2) The bounds checks at builtin_methods.c:1214 (write_u16), 1243 (write_u32), 1333 (write_u64), 1368 (write_i64) all use `(size_t)args[0].as.int_val + N > obj->as.buffer.len`. A positive 64-bit index like 4294967293 passes the `<0` test; on wasm32 (size_t=32-bit, the only build target where size_t is 32-bit — `#ifdef __EMSCRIPTEN__` in wasm_api.c) the cast truncates to 0xFFFFFFFD and `+4` wraps mod 2^32 to 1, which is <= len(16), so the check passes. Then `size_t i = (size_t)args[0].as.int_val = 0xFFFFFFFD` and `data[i]` resolves via 32-bit pointer arithmetic to data-3, giving a 3+1 byte attacker-controlled write underflowing the allocation. (3) The RegVM inline paths (regvm.c:2302 write_u16, 2331 write_u32) have the identical `(size_t)idx + N < len` truncation flaw. (4) Reachability is end-to-end: lat_run_line -> stackvm_run -> dispatch at stackvm.c:2483 -> builtin_buffer_write_u32 (args[0]=index, no clamping); lat_run_line_regvm -> regvm_run -> inline path. (5) Buffer::new(16) -> value_buffer_alloc(16) sets len=16, cap=16 (runtime.c:375, value.c:369-375), so the wrapped value 1>16 is false. 64-bit hosts are safe: (size_t)4294967293+4 = 4294967297 > 16 -> rejected. The finder did not misread ownership/bounds/types and there is no pre-existing guard. Severity high is appropriate: a controlled-byte OOB write reachable from fully untrusted Lattice source at the exact WASM trust boundary the sandbox exists to defend. Mitigating context worth noting: it affects only the wasm32 build (native 64-bit unaffected), corruption is contained within the wasm linear-memory sandbox (no host escape), and the per-write underflow window is narrow (the few bytes immediately preceding the buffer, i.e. allocator metadata / adjacent-allocation tail) — but a controlled write primitive over allocator metadata is a genuine escalation primitive, so high stands. || [high/high] I tried to refute this and could not. The mechanism holds under C semantics on wasm32.

Confirmed code: include/value.h:56 declares `int64_t int_val` (index arg keeps its full 64-bit value); include/value.h:111-112 declare the buffer as `uint8_t *data; size_t len`. On wasm32 `size_t` and pointers are 32-bit.

StackVM (src/builtin_methods.c:1243, write_u32): `if (... || (size_t)args[0].as.int_val + 4 > obj->as.buffer.len)`. With the trigger `b.write_u32(4294967293, 305419896)`, int_val = 0xFFFFFFFD: it is not `< 0` (positive int64); `(size_t)` truncates to 0xFFFFFFFD; `+4` overflows 32-bit to 1; `1 > 16` is false, so the guard is bypassed. Then `size_t i = 0xFFFFFFFD` and `data[i]..data[i+3]` resolve under 32-bit pointer arithmetic to `data-3 .. data+0` — a 3-byte attacker-controlled heap-underflow WRITE. Identical pattern at 1214 (u16,+2), 1333 (u64,+8), 1368 (i64,+8). RegVM inline (src/regvm.c:2331, write_u32): `(size_t)idx + 3 < len`; idx=0xFFFFFFFD gives `0 < 16` true → same underflow write; same at 2302 (u16).

Reachability: Makefile:280-289 compiles builtin_methods.c and regvm.c into the WASM target with no -sMEMORY64 (emscripten default = wasm32, 32-bit size_t), and exports _lat_run_line/_lat_run_line_regvm. src/wasm_api.c:87/246 lex→parse→compile→run untrusted `const char *source`, so untrusted Lattice source reaches these builtins. The attacker can also compute the index at runtime (e.g. 4294967296-3), so literal parsing is not a barrier.

64-bit hosts are safe (size_t 64-bit: 0xFFFFFFFD+4 = 0x100000001 > 16 rejects, and a non-negative int64 index +N cannot wrap 64-bit size_t), so the defect is genuinely wasm32-only, exactly as claimed.

Severity: a precise, attacker-controlled OOB heap write reachable from the intended untrusted entry point of a shipped artifact (the wasm playground) warrants high. I did not raise it to critical because WASM linear memory is sandboxed from the host — the corruption is contained to the module's own linear memory (no direct host RCE), though it is still a strong memory-corruption primitive within the sandbox (allocator-metadata/adjacent-allocation corruption). Confidence high: the integer-promotion/pointer-arithmetic analysis is conclusive and matches the reported ASan reproduction; the only thing I did not personally do is rerun emcc+ASan.

confirmed.title: 32-bit wraparound in Buffer.read_u16/u32/u64/i16/i32/i64/f32/f64 bounds check → OOB read / heap memory disclosure (wasm32)
confirmed.trigger: `flux b = Buffer::new(16); let x = b.read_u64(4294967288)` via lat_run_line / lat_run_line_regvm. Same 32-bit truncation+overflow as the write variant: `(size_t)idx + N` wraps below len so the check passes, then `obj->as.buffer.data + i` (i ~0xFFFFFFF8) wraps to a pointer just before the buffer and reads N bytes of out-of-bounds heap memory.
confirmed.consequence: Out-of-bounds READ of adjacent wasm linear-memory heap contents, with the leaked bytes returned to the caller as an Int/Float. An attacker running untrusted Lattice source can exfiltrate adjacent heap data (other objects, pointers useful for defeating ASLR within the module, secrets) back across the WASM boundary to JS. Same wasm32-only mechanism, mirrored in both VMs.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] VERIFIED REACHABLE. The WASM entry points lat_run_line (src/wasm_api.c:87) and lat_run_line_regvm (src/wasm_api.c:246) execute arbitrary JS-supplied Lattice source with no sandbox over which builtins may be invoked — this is the shipped public web playground (Makefile WASM_OUT=lattice-lang.org/lattice.js). Buffer::new is a real builtin constructor (src/eval.c:3540) and read_u64/read_u32/etc. are real builtin methods (parity-tested in tests/test_stdlib.c:10632+), so `flux b = Buffer::new(16); b.read_u64(4294967288)` is directly expressible by untrusted input. The StackVM pops the index off the stack and passes it unmodified to builtin_buffer_read_u64 (src/stackvm.c:2533-2540); the RegVM inlines the same check (src/regvm.c:2287/2313/2357/2372/2387/2402). The only gate is the in-builtin bounds check, and it is defeated on wasm32: args[0].as.int_val is int64_t (include/value.h:56) and obj->as.buffer.len is size_t; the WASM build is default wasm32 (Makefile:285-289 has no -sMEMORY64) so size_t/pointers are 32-bit. For idx=0xFFFFFFF8, the `<0` check passes (positive int64), `(size_t)idx` truncates to 0xFFFFFFF8, `+8` wraps to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic, `0 > 16` is false → check passes; then `obj->as.buffer.data + i` (i=0xFFFFFFF8) wraps to data-8 and reads 8 OOB bytes returned to the script (src/builtin_methods.c:1319/1324-1328 for u64, mirrored for u16/u32/i16/i32/i64/f32/f64). On native 64-bit the same expression is correctly rejected (4294967296 > 16), so this is genuinely wasm32-only, matching the cited subsystem. No upstream validator clamps the index. Severity adjusted from high to medium: it is a real, reachable memory-safety violation, but read-only (information disclosure, not corruption), wasm32-specific, and the OOB window is bounded to heap immediately preceding the buffer (~[data-N, data-N+len]) rather than truly arbitrary memory; in the as-shipped per-tab playground the disclosed bytes belong to the attacker's own session, so the cross-trust-boundary secret-exfiltration framing only fully applies to a hypothetical embedder running untrusted scripts alongside sensitive host data in shared linear memory. It does compose with the parallel write-side 32-bit truncation in the same builtins to form a read+write primitive. || [high/high] Mechanism re-derived from real code and confirmed. confirmed.title: String.pad_left/pad_right: 64-bit length guard vs 32-bit-truncated malloc → giant OOB memset write (wasm32)
confirmed.trigger: `"abcd".pad_left(4294967296, "x")` (RegVM also via `"abcd".pad_left(4294967296)`) through lat_run_line / lat_run_line_regvm. The guard `(int64_t)slen >= n` compares against the full 64-bit n (2^32) and proceeds, but `(size_t)n` truncates to 0 on wasm32, so `malloc((size_t)n + 1)` allocates 1 byte while `plen = (size_t)n - slen = 0 - 4` underflows to ~4 GiB, and `memset(buf, pad, plen)` runs off the end of the allocation. On 64-bit hosts malloc((size_t)n+1) is ~4 GiB and fails, returning a clone — safe; only wasm32 truncation makes it exploitable.
confirmed.consequence: Massive out-of-bounds memset WRITE past a 1-byte heap allocation (verified with AddressSanitizer on the real wasm build: `negative-size-param: (size=-4)` in builtin_string_pad_left, stack lat_run_line→stackvm_run→builtin_string_pad_left). Reliable heap corruption / crash (DoS) driven purely by an untrusted integer argument at the WASM boundary; mirrored in both VMs and in pad_right.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] REACHABILITY CONFIRMED. The cited code matches reality. builtin_string_pad_left (src/builtin_methods.c:903-916), builtin_string_pad_right (918-930), and the RegVM inline versions (src/regvm.c:1883-1899, 1901-1917) all use the width-mismatched pattern: guard `if ((int64_t)slen >= n) return clone` compares the full 64-bit n, but `plen = (size_t)n - slen` and `malloc((size_t)n + 1)` use a size_t-truncated n. On wasm32 (emscripten: 32-bit size_t/pointers), `(size_t)4294967296` truncates to 0, so malloc(0+1)=malloc(1) succeeds while plen = 0u-4 underflows to ~4 GiB, and `memset(buf, pad, plen)` runs off the 1-byte allocation. The guard is bypassed because 4 >= 2^32 is false. On 64-bit hosts the bug is latent (malloc ~4 GiB fails → returns clone), so it is genuinely wasm32-specific, exactly as the finder scopes it.

I traced the full untrusted-input path and found NO upstream guard that blocks the trigger:
1. lat_run_line / lat_run_line_regvm (src/wasm_api.c:87, 246) accept arbitrary source from the JS caller (the WASM playground) and run it directly through lex→parse→compile→VM with no value sandboxing.
2. The lexer parses the literal 4294967296 via strtoll into int64 (src/lexer.c:210); it only rejects ERANGE values exceeding int64, so 2^32 is a valid token — no clamp/truncation.
3. The compiler emits it as a full int64 constant (src/stackcompiler.c:514, value_int) since it exceeds the int8 fast-path range; method-call expressions are not constant-folded, so it dispatches at runtime.
4. StackVM generic builtin dispatch (src/stackvm.c:1941-1951, arg_count==2) and RegVM dispatch (src/regvm.c:1883, arg_count 1 or 2) call the vulnerable builtins with args[0].as.int_val = 4294967296 intact. (pic_resolve_builtin_id at stackvm.c:1165 is only inline-cache metadata; the actual call always reaches the builtin.) So `"abcd".pad_left(4294967296, "x")` (StackVM) and `"abcd".pad_left(4294967296)` (RegVM) reach the OOB memset. Bug is real and mirrored across both VMs and pad_right.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADE high→medium. It is a genuine reachable OOB heap write (memory-safety defect), so not-a-bug is wrong. However, the threat model bounds the impact: the affected surface is the wasm32 playground, an arbitrary-code-execution sandbox where the "untrusted" input is code the user themselves authors and runs in their own session. The OOB write is a single libc memset of a constant byte that cannot be stopped mid-stream and that, in a wasm32 module, is bounds-checked against linear memory — it will overwrite the heap above buf and then trap at the linear-memory boundary rather than escaping to host memory. Net effect is a reliable self-inflicted DoS/crash within the user's own sandbox, not a sandbox escape, cross-user compromise, or controllable corruption primitive. That is solidly a real memory-safety bug worth fixing (medium), not a high-impact security vulnerability. || [high/high] VERIFIED REAL against actual source. The cited code matches exactly. builtin_methods.c:905-915 (pad_left) and 920-929 (pad_right): n is int64_t from args[0].as.int_val; the bounds guard `if ((int64_t)slen >= n) return value_deep_clone(obj);` compares against the FULL 64-bit n, but the allocation `malloc((size_t)n + 1)` and the padding length `plen = (size_t)n - slen` both use the SIZE_T-truncated value. RegVM regvm.c:1884-1898 / 1902-1916 mirrors this identically (and via `arg_count == 1 || arg_count == 2` the single-arg form also triggers it).

Supporting facts all confirmed: (1) Integer literals are parsed with strtoll into int64_t (lexer.c:210), range-checked only against int64 ERANGE, so 4294967296 (2^32) is preserved exactly — no 32-bit clamp. (3) Reachable from the untrusted WASM entry: lat_run_line (wasm_api.c:87) → stackvm_run → dispatch at stackvm.c:1941 calls builtin_string_pad_left with the user-controlled n; lat_run_line_regvm (wasm_api.c:246) → regvm.c:1883.

The reported ASan signature `negative-size-param: (size=-4)` is precisely (size_t)0 - 4 on a 32-bit target, internally consistent with the truncation. On 64-bit hosts the bug does not manifest (malloc of ~4 GiB fails → clone, or succeeds with correct in-bounds memset) — so it is wasm32-specific, exactly as claimed. The finder did not misread ownership, types, bounds, or any existing check; there is no upstream argument-range validation. Severity: high is appropriate — an untrusted-integer-driven, reliable OOB heap write at the sandbox boundary causing heap corruption / trap (DoS). It writes only a constant pad byte and wasm linear-memory bounds will eventually trap, so it is not a controlled-content arbitrary-write RCE; high (not critical) is the right call. || [high/high] Confirmed real on the named wasm32 boundary. (2) The guard does NOT catch it because the comparison is done in 64-bit space. (3) size_t IS 32-bit on the target: Makefile:783-789 builds with emcc, no -sMEMORY64, i.e. default wasm32. So (uint32_t)2^32 = 0 → malloc(1) (1 byte), while plen = (size_t)0 - 4 = 0xFFFFFFFC (~4 GiB), and memset(buf, pad, plen) overruns the 1-byte allocation by ~4 GiB. StackVM needs arg_count==2 (`"abcd".pad_left(4294967296, "x")`); RegVM accepts arg_count 1 or 2 (regvm.c:1883), matching the claimed triggers exactly. On 64-bit hosts size_t is 64-bit, so malloc(~4 GiB) fails and the code returns a clone (safe) — consistent with the claim that only wasm32 is exploitable.

Realistic impact is deterministic heap corruption followed by a wasm trap (DoS) rather than a finely controlled write primitive (length is enormous, contents are a fixed pad byte, cannot escape the sandbox), but it remains a clear memory-safety violation, mirrored across both VMs and both pad directions.

confirmed.title: Use-after-free: s_varrefs realloc invalidates the value pointer held by build_compound_variables/handle_variables during variable-tree expansion
confirmed.trigger: Stop at a breakpoint, then send a DAP `variables` request to expand a compound value (array/struct/tuple) that contains roughly >=31 nested compound children. `build_compound_variables` receives `val == &ref->value` which points into the global `s_varrefs` array. While iterating, each nested compound child calls `get_varref_for_value -> varrefs_add`, and when `s_varref_count` reaches `s_varref_cap` the `realloc(s_varrefs)` moves the buffer, leaving `val` (and the caller's `ref`) dangling. The next loop iteration reads `val->as.array.len` / `val->as.array.elems[i]` from freed memory. Reproduced: global `let g = [[0],[1],...,[39]]`, breakpoint, expand g -> ASAN heap-use-after-free READ of size 8 at dap.c:638 (build_compound_variables), freed by the realloc in varrefs_add.
confirmed.consequence: Heap-use-after-free read on the pure variable-display path (no `evaluate` needed). A normal or malicious DAP client triggers it simply by expanding a moderately large nested structure in the debugger's variables view. The dangling `elems` pointer is then dereferenced in value_repr, giving a wild read / crash / potential info disclosure depending on heap reuse.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] CONFIRMED REAL and REACHABLE from untrusted DAP-client input. Full chain verified by reading source:

REACHABILITY: `--dap` (main.c:1507) sets dap_mode; the regvm path returns at main.c:311, so a debugger is only ever attached to the StackVM (main.c:344-348) — the (StackVM*) casts in dap.c are never handed a RegVM, matching the prompt's note. During `stackvm_run`, every instruction calls `debugger_check` (stackvm.c:3360), which on a pause delegates to `dap_debugger_check` (debugger.c:552). That function (dap.c:557) runs a message loop reading DAP requests from `dbg->dap_in` = stdin — i.e. arbitrary protocol messages from the DAP client. A `variables` request (dap.c:632-638) routes to `handle_variables` (dap.c:354).

UAF MECHANICS: For a compound var_ref, handle_variables does `ref = varrefs_find(var_ref)` returning `&s_varrefs[idx]` (dap.c:48), then `build_compound_variables(&ref->value)` (dap.c:366) — so `val` points INTO the global `s_varrefs` buffer. The array branch (dap.c:225-237) re-reads `val->as.array.len` (line 226) and `val->as.array.elems[i]` (lines 231,234) every iteration and calls `get_varref_for_value` -> `varrefs_add` (dap.c:32-42) for each compound child. `varrefs_add` does `realloc(s_varrefs, ...)` when `s_varref_count >= s_varref_cap` (cap 0->32->64...; varrefs_clear only zeroes count, not cap). When a single expansion pushes the count across the cap boundary mid-loop, realloc moves the buffer and `val` (plus the caller's `ref`) dangle; the next loop iteration's reads of `val->as.array.len`/`elems` and the subsequent `value_repr` deref of the dangling `elems` are heap-use-after-free reads.

NO GUARDS: grep confirms the only cap/realloc logic is the unguarded doubling at dap.c:33-35; build_compound_variables has no depth/size limit and never re-fetches `val`. The finder's repro (g=[[0]..[39]], expand g) crosses 32 during one loop. The ASAN "dap.c:638" line is just the inlined static-function call site (handle_variables), consistent with build_compound_variables being inlined — not a discrepancy.

SEVERITY: Genuine UAF read with a dangling pointer subsequently dereferenced in value_repr (potential wild read / crash / heap info disclosure). It is gated behind an explicit `--dap` debug session rather than normal `clat file.lat` execution, which limits the attack surface — but expanding a moderately large nested array in the variables view is an entirely ordinary debugger action, so the only real precondition is "the debugger is in use." Keeping high (memory-safety UAF reachable through normal debugger interaction); a case for medium exists purely on the debug-mode gating. || [high/high] The mechanism holds exactly as claimed, verified against the real code in src/dap.c.

1. Aliasing into s_varrefs is real. handle_variables (dap.c:364-366) does `DapVarRef *ref = varrefs_find(var_ref); ... build_compound_variables(&ref->value);`. varrefs_find (dap.c:44-49) returns `&s_varrefs[idx]`, so `&ref->value` is a pointer DIRECTLY into the heap-allocated s_varrefs buffer. No copy is made — build_compound_variables takes `LatValue *val` and dereferences `*val` throughout its loop.

2. The loop re-reads *val every iteration. build_compound_variables (dap.c:225-237) for VAL_ARRAY: `for (size_t i = 0; i < val->as.array.len; i++)` and inside reads `val->as.array.elems[i]` (confirmed fields exist in include/value.h:63-67: `struct { LatValue *elems; size_t len; size_t cap; } array`).

3. Each nested compound child reallocs s_varrefs. For each element it calls `get_varref_for_value(&val->as.array.elems[i])` (dap.c:234). get_varref_for_value (dap.c:136-141) calls varrefs_add for VAL_ARRAY/STRUCT/MAP/TUPLE. varrefs_add (dap.c:32-42) grows reactively: `if (s_varref_count >= s_varref_cap) { s_varref_cap = s_varref_cap ? s_varref_cap*2 : 32; s_varrefs = realloc(s_varrefs, ...); }`. realloc can (and under ASAN always does) move the buffer, freeing the old region that `val` still points into.

4. Trigger is sound. On a fresh process s_varref_cap is a static = 0. varrefs_clear (dap.c:30) only resets count, not cap. To obtain var_ref 1000 for `g`, the client must first expand GLOBALS (build_globals_variables -> get_varref_for_value(&g) -> varrefs_add), which makes count=1 and grows cap 0->32. Then expanding g (var_ref 1000) enters build_compound_variables with val = &s_varrefs[0].value. The 40 nested arrays push count from 1; at i=31 (count 32 >= cap 32) varrefs_add reallocs to cap 64, moving the buffer and dangling `val`. Iterations i=32..39 then read `val->as.array.len` (loop condition) and `val->as.array.elems[i]` (value_repr) from freed memory — a heap-use-after-free READ, matching the claimed ASAN reproduction.

I checked for guards and found none: no defensive copy of the value before the loop, no pre-sizing of s_varrefs, no reentrancy/snapshot. The backend-cast lens is correctly noted as irrelevant here (no RegVM involved). The category 'uaf' is correct.

Severity: kept at high. It is a genuine, ASAN-reproducible heap-use-after-free reachable on the pure variable-display path via standard DAP `variables` requests, and the freed DapVarRef.value.array.elems pointer is subsequently dereferenced by value_repr (after potential heap reuse this is a wild read -> crash / info disclosure). The only mitigating factor is that it requires the opt-in --dap debugger mode and a breakpoint hit (a developer-facing tool, read-only), which one could argue down to medium; but as a clear memory-safety UAF it warrants high. || [high/high] CONFIRMED real use-after-free; could not refute. The aliasing chain is concrete in source: handle_variables (src/dap.c:364-366) calls build_compound_variables(&ref->value) where ref = varrefs_find(var_ref) returns &s_varrefs[idx] (dap.c:44-49). So the `val` pointer passed to build_compound_variables points INTO the global s_varrefs heap buffer; no defensive copy is made (build_compound_variables takes a bare LatValue*). Inside the loop (dap.c:225-237) it re-reads `val->as.array.len` (loop condition) and `val->as.array.elems[i]` through `val` every iteration (struct layout confirmed at include/value.h:64-66), and for each compound child calls get_varref_for_value (dap.c:136-141) -> varrefs_add(*val), which does realloc(s_varrefs) whenever s_varref_count >= s_varref_cap (dap.c:33-36, cap starts 32 and doubles). When the count crosses the cap mid-loop the buffer is moved and `val` dangles; the subsequent iteration reads len/elems[i] from freed memory.

Refutation attempts all failed: (a) no copy of the value — pointer is passed directly; (b) loop genuinely re-loads through `val` each iteration, not a cached length; (c) varrefs_clear (dap.c:30, invoked only on stop at dap.c:564) only zeroes the count and retains the buffer, so within a single stop the count grows monotonically across successive `variables` requests, allowing it to reach 32 during an expansion; (d) no bound, null check, length validation, or reentrancy guard guards this path; (e) the backend-cast lens is irrelevant here (StackVM-only), matching the prompt's note. Trigger is reachable on the ordinary variable-display path: list globals (pre-seeds the count), then expand a compound with >=~31 compound children (e.g. g=[[0]..[39]]); around the 32nd varrefs_add the realloc moves the buffer and the next loop iteration is a heap-use-after-free READ.

The only standard-level caveat is that realloc is not *required* to relocate on growth, but the C standard permits it and ASAN relocates deterministically (the finder reproduced the ASAN heap-use-after-free), so the code is genuinely unsafe.

Severity: it is a true memory-safety UAF (read) reachable on a normal debugger workflow with no malicious input required, so high is appropriate; I note exposure is bounded because the DAP debugger is a local developer-driven tool (not a network-facing service), but reliable UAF crashes / potential info disclosure on ordinary usage keep this at high rather than medium.

confirmed.title: Unbounded stack buffer overflow in REPL line accumulation (strcat into fixed accumulated[65536])
confirmed.location: /Users/alexjokela/projects/lattice/src/main.c:601, 612-613 (identical bug also at 752/763-764 in repl_regvm and 889/900-901 in repl_tree_walk)
confirmed.trigger: Run the REPL (`./clat`, or pipe a file: `./clat < script`). The line returned by readline()/editline (which the default build links — libedit confirmed via otool) is unbounded. Two reachable paths: (1) a single input line longer than 65535 bytes is strcat'd directly into accumulated[65536]; (2) input_is_complete() returns false while bracket depth>0, so repeated unbalanced lines are appended across iterations and accumulated is never reset, growing without bound. Both confirmed at runtime: a 70000-char line crashed clat with exit 133 (SIGTRAP / stack-canary), a 60000-char line ran cleanly (exit 0), and 40000 unbalanced `{` lines also crashed with exit 133.
confirmed.consequence: Out-of-bounds write past a 64 KiB stack buffer with fully attacker-controlled length and content (the raw pasted/piped line bytes). On the default build the compiler-inserted stack canary turns this into a guaranteed crash (denial of service) — note pasting a large code snippet into the REPL is an ordinary user action. The Makefile CFLAGS do not request -fstack-protector; on toolchains/platforms where the canary is absent this is a classic stack smash that overwrites the saved return address, enabling memory corruption and potential code execution.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/high] Confirmed by reading src/main.c. `char accumulated[65536];` (line 601) is written by unbounded `strcat(accumulated, "\n")` / `strcat(accumulated, line)` at lines 612-613, with no length check on the line or on the accumulated total. The same defect is duplicated at 763-764 (repl_regvm) and 900-901 (repl_tree_walk). The path is fully reachable from REPL stdin: `readline()` (line 606) -> `strcat` (612-613). The default macOS/Linux build links libedit (Makefile:36 sets -DLATTICE_HAS_EDITLINE via `pkg-config --cflags libedit`), whose readline() returns unbounded-length lines, so a single pasted/piped line >65535 bytes overflows on the first strcat. Independently, `input_is_complete()` (lines 545-571) only checks bracket balance; while depth>0 it returns false, the loop `continue`s, and `accumulated` is never reset (it is cleared only after a complete or errored input at lines 627/641/662/710), so repeated unbalanced lines accumulate without bound and overflow even the 4095-byte-capped fallback readline (lines 35-47). I searched upstream for guards and found none: there is no length validation, no bounded copy (snprintf/strlcat), no accumulated-length cap, and no truncation between input and the strcat. Makefile:2 CFLAGS (`-std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -Iinclude -O3 -DVM_USE_COMPUTED_GOTO`) does not request -fstack-protector, so on toolchains without a default canary this is an unguarded stack smash overwriting the saved return address with attacker-controlled length and content; on canary-enabled default toolchains it is a guaranteed crash/DoS (matches the reported exit 133). This is a genuine, reachable OOB stack write. Severity remains high: it is unguarded attacker-controlled memory corruption, with the only mitigating factor being that the input vector is the interactive/piped REPL (pasted snippet or `./clat < script`) rather than a remote protocol message. || [high/high] Mechanism verified against actual code. src/main.c:601 declares `char accumulated[65536]` (a fixed 64 KiB stack buffer); lines 612-613 perform `strcat(accumulated, "\n")` then `strcat(accumulated, line)` with zero bounds checking. The same pattern is duplicated verbatim in repl_regvm (752/763-764) and repl_tree_walk (889/900-901).

Two reachable overflow paths confirmed:
(1) Single oversized line. confirmed.title: Quadratic output-buffer blowup from accumulated indentation on nested braces (memory-exhaustion DoS)
confirmed.trigger: Format a source document containing deeply nested / many sequential open braces, e.g. a file consisting of N '{' characters. Each '{' increments f.indent and starts a new line; emit_indent then emits 4*indent spaces per line, so total output = sum 4*(k-1) = 2*N*(N-1) = O(N^2). Empirically measured against the standalone ASAN build: N=500->500000 bytes, N=1000->2000000, N=2000->8000000, N=4000->32000000, N=8000->128000000 (exact quadratic). A ~200KB malicious source (~200000 '{') expands to ~80GB of output.
confirmed.consequence: Unbounded O(N^2) output-buffer growth (LatStr doubled via realloc) drives memory exhaustion and CPU blowup from a tiny input. On the long-lived LSP server this OOM-kills/hangs the language server for the editing session; for the CLI it OOM-kills the process. A few-hundred-KB source is enough to exhaust many GB. No size guard exists on any of the three entry points.

For N sequential '{', the k-th brace lands at_line_start and emits 4*(k-1) spaces, giving total = 4*sum(0..N-1) = 2*N*(N-1) = O(N^2). The output buffer is a LatStr that doubles via realloc (src/ds/str.c lat_str_grow), so a tiny input produces a huge allocation. No cap exists anywhere in the format path: lat_format (formatter.c:352) just loops while f.pos < f.len; f.len = strlen(source).

1. CLI `clat fmt <file>` (src/main.c:1446/1469): read_file (main.c:52-71) reads the entire file via ftell/fread with no size limit, then calls lat_format directly.
2. CLI `clat fmt --stdin` (main.c:1430): lat_format_stdin (formatter.c:1154-1183) slurps all of stdin into a doubling buffer with no cap, then lat_format.

(4) A later variables request for that varref -> build_compound_variables(&ref->value) (dap.c:222-237) iterates val->as.array.len (valid copied scalar) and dereferences the dangling val->as.array.elems[i], calling value_repr (value.c:1369) on freed memory = heap-use-after-free read. No deep clone, refcount, or guard breaks the chain; the aliasing and the free both check out against the real code. The backend-cast caveat is irrelevant (debugger attaches only to StackVM). Severity downgraded conceptually to medium because the bug is confined to the DAP debug-adapter feature requiring an active debug session and a specific client-driven message sequence (expand compound var, reassign via console, re-expand); it is not reachable during normal program execution and is developer-tooling-facing, not a production/network path. Matches the claimed medium rating. || [high/medium] VERIFIED REAL — could not refute. - dap.c:456→debugger.c:240: `handle_evaluate`→`debugger_eval_expr`→`stackvm_run(vm,chunk,&result)` executes arbitrary user code (e.g. `g = 0`) on the live VM.

The region early-return at value.c:1553 only fires when `region_id != (size_t)-1`; a mutable top-level array global is REGION_NONE (`(size_t)-1`, per the default at value.c:253 and OP_DEFINE_GLOBAL promotion to REGION_NONE), so the buffer is genuinely freed.

It is confined to DAP debug mode (a developer-facing tool, not a production execution path), which is why it is not higher than medium; one could argue low on that basis, but a client-triggerable UAF justifies medium. The backend-cast lens is correctly excluded — the debugger only ever sees the StackVM.

confirmed.title: NULL-pointer dereference when DAP request `command` is present but not a JSON string
confirmed.code:
dap_handshake (dap.c:484-490):
    cJSON *cmd = cJSON_GetObjectItem(msg, "command");
    ...
    const char *command = cmd ? cmd->valuestring : "";   /* cmd->valuestring is NULL for non-string items */
    ...
    if (strcmp(command, "initialize") == 0) {            /* strcmp(NULL, ...) -> SIGSEGV */

/* Same pattern in dap_debugger_check (dap.c:587/590): const char *command = cmd ? cmd->valuestring : ""; then strcmp(command, "continue") */
confirmed.trigger: Send a DAP message whose `type` is "request" and whose `command` field is any non-string JSON value, e.g. `{"type":"request","seq":1,"command":123}` or `{"type":"request","seq":1,"command":null}`. cJSON sets `valuestring` to NULL for number/null/bool/array/object items, so the `cmd ? cmd->valuestring : ""` guard passes through NULL, and the first `strcmp(command, ...)` dereferences NULL. Reproduced under ASAN: SEGV on address 0x0 in strcmp called from dap_handshake dap.c:490. Control: command absent or a valid string does not crash.
confirmed.consequence: Remote/untrusted DAP input (editor JSON-RPC) crashes the debug adapter via NULL dereference at the very first request (handshake) and at every stop in the message loop. Denial of service of the debugger from a single malformed message.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] CONFIRMED REAL and REACHABLE. Code: src/dap.c:484-490 in dap_handshake does `cJSON *cmd = cJSON_GetObjectItem(msg, "command"); ... const char *command = cmd ? cmd->valuestring : ""; ... if (strcmp(command, "initialize") == 0)`. The ternary only checks item presence, not JSON type. cJSON behavior verified in the vendored source: cJSON_New_Item (vendor/cJSON.c:241-249) memsets the node to zero, and parse_number (line 307-386) sets only valuedouble/valueint, never valuestring; string parsing sets valuestring at line 926. Therefore a number/null/bool/array/object item has valuestring==NULL, so `command` becomes NULL and `strcmp(NULL, "initialize")` dereferences NULL -> SIGSEGV. The identical pattern recurs at dap.c:587/590 in dap_debugger_check.

Reachability from untrusted input is established end-to-end: main.c:1507 `--dap` sets dap_mode; main.c:344-345 calls debugger_new_dap(stdin, stdout); debugger.c:64 sets dbg->dap_in = stdin; dap_handshake (dap.c:470) loops reading dap_read_message(dbg->dap_in), which at dap.c:53-77 reads a Content-Length-framed body off the stream and feeds it to cJSON_Parse with no type validation. This is exactly the editor's DAP JSON-RPC stream — untrusted/external input. The trigger {"type":"request","seq":1,"command":123} passes the type=="request" guard at line 479 (type is a genuine string item) and then hits command==NULL at line 487 and the crashing strcmp at line 490. {"type":"request","command":null} crashes identically. No upstream guard rejects a non-string command anywhere on this path.

Corroborating that this is an oversight rather than intended: the disconnect-drain loop at dap.c:684 uses the correct guard `cmd && cmd->valuestring && strcmp(...)`, while lines 487 and 587 omit the valuestring check.

Severity medium is appropriate: a single malformed message NULL-dereferences and crashes the debug adapter (DoS) at the first handshake request and at every stop, but it is confined to the opt-in --dap developer tooling and is a NULL read, not exploitable memory corruption. Not critical/high. Fix: guard with cJSON_IsString(cmd) or `cmd && cmd->valuestring` at lines 487 and 587 (and 490/590 callers). || [high/medium] Mechanism re-derived from actual code and confirmed real.

src/dap.c:484-490 (dap_handshake) and the identical block at 584-590 (dap_debugger_check):
  cJSON *cmd = cJSON_GetObjectItem(msg, "command");
  ...
  const char *command = cmd ? cmd->valuestring : "";   // line 487 / 587
  ...
  if (strcmp(command, "initialize") == 0) { ... }       // line 490 / 590

The guard only checks whether the item `cmd` is non-NULL, not whether its `valuestring` is non-NULL. I verified cJSON's behavior in vendor/cJSON.c:
- cJSON_New_Item (cJSON.c:246) does memset(node,'\0',sizeof(cJSON)), so valuestring is initialized NULL.
- parse_number sets valuedouble/valueint but never assigns valuestring; null/bool/array/object parsing likewise leave it NULL. Only string parsing (parse_string) assigns valuestring.

So for a request with a non-string command (e.g. {"type":"request","seq":1,"command":123} or "command":null), cJSON_GetObjectItem returns a non-NULL item whose valuestring == NULL. The ternary therefore yields NULL (cmd is truthy), and strcmp(NULL, "initialize") at line 490 dereferences NULL → SIGSEGV. The trigger description is accurate: type must be the valid string "request" to pass the line-479 type guard, and command must be a non-string.

Control cases confirm the diagnosis: command absent → cmd NULL → "" (safe); command:"" → valid empty string → falls through to the else dap_send_error branch (safe). The bug is specifically the non-string-present case.

Strong corroborating evidence that this is a real oversight rather than a misread: line 479 (and 579) explicitly guards the analogous field with `!type->valuestring` before strcmp — the author already knows valuestring can be NULL for non-string JSON — but the equivalent `!cmd->valuestring` / `cmd->valuestring &&` guard is missing for command at 487/587.

Reachability: dap_read_message reads JSON-RPC from dbg->dap_in (the editor/DAP client stream), and dap_handshake runs at the very first request, so a single malformed message crashes the adapter immediately; the loop in dap_debugger_check repeats the hazard at every stop. The backend-cast caveat is correctly noted and irrelevant here.

Severity: kept at medium. It is a genuine NULL-deref DoS reachable from protocol input, but the DAP peer is normally the user's own editor over stdio (not truly remote/network-untrusted), and the debug adapter is an opt-in tool, which bounds practical impact. Medium is a fair characterization; one could argue low on the trust model, but the crash itself is unambiguous and trivially triggerable by any malformed/buggy client or fuzzer. || [high/medium] Confirmed real by reading src/dap.c and vendor/cJSON.c. At dap.c:487 and 587 the guard is `const char *command = cmd ? cmd->valuestring : "";` which only protects against the `command` field being absent (cmd==NULL), NOT against cmd being a present non-string item whose valuestring is NULL. cJSON_New_Item (cJSON.c:241) memset-zeros nodes so valuestring is NULL by default; parse_value (cJSON.c:1363) only sets valuestring via parse_string for `"`-quoted input — for numbers (parse_number), null/true/false, arrays, and objects valuestring stays NULL. Therefore a message like {"type":"request","seq":1,"command":123} produces a non-NULL cmd with cmd->valuestring==NULL, the guard passes NULL through, and the immediately following strcmp(command,"initialize") (dap.c:490) / strcmp(command,"continue") (dap.c:590) dereferences NULL -> SIGSEGV. The bug is an inconsistency in the file's own defensive style: the type field is guarded correctly at dap.c:479/579 (`!type->valuestring`), and the third message loop at dap.c:684 guards correctly (`cmd && cmd->valuestring && strcmp(...)`); only the two cited sites omit the cmd->valuestring check. dap_read_message (dap.c:53) does no schema validation, so the malformed message reaches the loop unfiltered, and the handshake site is the very first request processed. Severity medium is correct: it is a NULL-pointer-dereference denial-of-service of the debug adapter from a single malformed DAP message, with no memory corruption or code execution, and reachable only when running in DAP/debug-adapter mode.

confirmed.title: Untrusted search path: CWD-relative ./extensions/ dlopen preferred over user/system dirs (library planting -> arbitrary code execution)
confirmed.trigger: Run any Lattice script that calls require_ext("<name>") while the process CWD is a directory an attacker can write to. ext_load builds its search list with the two CWD-relative ./extensions/ paths FIRST and dlopen()s the first one that exists, before ~/.lattice/ext and $LATTICE_EXT_PATH. An attacker who drops ./extensions/<name>.dylib (or .so/.dll) into the working directory gets that library loaded instead of the legitimately installed one. The name-validation only blocks '/' and '\\' (path traversal); it does not stop CWD planting.
confirmed.consequence: Arbitrary native code execution in the host process. Proven with a PoC: a planted ./extensions/evil.dylib both ran code in its __attribute__((constructor)) at dlopen time and returned attacker-controlled values from the registered function. Because the CWD entries are searched first, a malicious file also SHADOWS a trusted system-installed extension of the same name. Affects a victim running a benign require_ext script in a shared/temp/download directory.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Reachability is confirmed and unobstructed. require_ext(name) is a builtin reachable from any Lattice source file in both backends: tree-walker at src/eval.c:3317 and the runtime/VM native native_require_ext at src/runtime.c:3017 (registered src/runtime.c:4477). The string argument flows straight into ext_load (src/ext.c:241). The only upstream validation (src/ext.c:245-254) rejects empty names and names containing '/' or '\\' — that stops path traversal but does NOT stop CWD planting, because the attacker uses a bare token name and drops ./extensions/<name>.dylib. The search list is built with literal CWD-relative strings: paths[0]="./extensions/<name><suffix>" (line 270) and paths[1]="./extensions/<name>/<name><suffix>" (line 273), built BEFORE ~/.lattice/ext (index 2, line 281) and $LATTICE_EXT_PATH (index 3, line 285). No realpath/chdir/script-dir normalization is applied (consistent with the project's documented CWD-relative import resolution). The dlopen loop (lines 296-299) iterates in order and breaks on the first hit, so a planted CWD library both executes (constructor at dlopen time and the unconditional init_fn call at line 331 -> arbitrary native code) and shadows a trusted user/system-installed extension of the same name. This is a real CWE-426/427 untrusted-search-path / library-planting defect leading to arbitrary code execution. Severity is medium rather than higher because exploitation requires the attacker to already have write access to the victim's working directory and the victim to run a require_ext script from there; the silent shadowing of trusted installs keeps it from being merely low. The finder's snippet, ordering claim, and trigger all match the actual code. || [high/medium] Mechanism re-derived from the real code in src/ext.c (ext_load, lines 241-300) and it holds exactly as claimed.

Search-list construction (path_count starts at 0):
- Line 270: paths[0] = "./extensions/<name><suffix>"
- Line 273: paths[1] = "./extensions/<name>/<name><suffix>"
- Line 281: paths[2] = "<HOME>/.lattice/ext/<name><suffix>" (only if HOME set)
- Line 285: paths[3] = "$LATTICE_EXT_PATH/<name><suffix>" (only if set)

Resolution loop (lines 296-299, mirrored for Win32 LoadLibraryA at 290-292): iterates i = 0..path_count and breaks on the first dlopen() that succeeds. The two CWD-relative entries are therefore tried strictly before the per-user (~/.lattice/ext) and env-configured (LATTICE_EXT_PATH) locations. Because "./extensions/<name>.dylib" contains slashes, dlopen treats it as a CWD-relative pathname (not a loader search), so a file planted at $CWD/extensions/<name>.dylib is loaded directly. On load, its __attribute__((constructor)) runs and its lat_ext_init() (invoked at line 331) registers attacker-controlled native functions — confirming arbitrary native code execution and shadowing of a legitimately installed extension of the same name.

The only input guard is line 251 (strchr(name,'/') || strchr(name,'\\')), which blocks path traversal embedded in the *name* but does nothing to prevent CWD planting — the "./extensions/" prefix is a fixed literal. There is no realpath check, no flag to disable CWD search, no ownership/trust check anywhere in the function. Buffer math is fine (paths[5][1024], max index 3, snprintf-bounded), so the finder's claim is purely about search-order/trust, not overflow, and that is accurate.

This is a textbook CWE-426 Untrusted Search Path / library-planting issue: searching a writable CWD for loadable native code before trusted user/system directories. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, types, or an existing check.

Severity: kept at medium rather than high because exploitation has real preconditions (victim must run a require_ext("<name>") script while CWD is attacker-writable, and the attacker must know/guess the extension name and platform suffix), and the CWD-first ordering is partly an intentional "local project extensions" convenience. But the consequence on trigger is full native RCE in the host process, which is plausible in shared/temp/download directories, so it is a genuine vulnerability worth fixing (e.g., demote or drop CWD search, require an explicit opt-in, or prefer trusted dirs first). || [high/medium] Confirmed by reading src/ext.c:241-300. ext_load() builds its dlopen search list with the two CWD-relative entries first and unconditionally: paths[0]="./extensions/<name><suffix>" (line 270) and paths[1]="./extensions/<name>/<name><suffix>" (line 273), before ~/.lattice/ext (line 281, only if $HOME set) and $LATTICE_EXT_PATH (line 285, only if set). The try-loop at lines 296-299 does `handle = dlopen(paths[i], RTLD_NOW); if (handle) break;`, so the first existing path wins — a CWD-planted ./extensions/<name>.{dylib,so,dll} is loaded in preference to, and thus shadows, any trusted home/env-installed extension of the same name. RTLD_NOW resolves eagerly and runs the library's constructor at load time, so merely loading the planted file already executes attacker code. The only input validation (lines 245-254) rejects empty names and names containing '/' or '\\' to stop path traversal; it does nothing to prevent CWD planting. Trigger path is genuine: require_ext is registered as a native (runtime.c:4477) and dispatched in eval.c:3317, calling ext_load with the script-supplied name verbatim. I searched for any guard (CWD ownership/writability check, absolute-path or script-dir restriction, secure-load flag, env-disable) and found none. As an active refuter I could not find a guard or invariant making this safe; the code does exactly what the finding states. This is a classic CWE-426/427 untrusted-search-path / library-planting weakness leading to arbitrary native code execution. Severity medium is justified: impact is full in-process code execution, but exploitation requires the victim to run a require_ext-using script with its CWD in an attacker-writable directory and the attacker to know/guess the extension name.

confirmed.title: Unbounded Content-Length: no upper cap -> huge malloc + blocking fread wedges the single-threaded server
confirmed.trigger:
Editor (untrusted per the threat model) sends a header frame such as `Content-Length: 2000000000

` and then either stalls or never delivers the full body. atoi() accepts any positive int up to INT_MAX; the only validation is `content_length <= 0`. The code then malloc's up to ~2GB and calls fread() for that many bytes. Confirmed at runtime: `{ printf 'Content-Length: 1000000

hello'; sleep 6; } | ./clat-lsp` leaves clat-lsp blocked in fread() after 2s (server still running, processing no further messages).
confirmed.consequence: Denial of service. The server is single-threaded (one message processed per loop iteration in lsp_server_run), so a single oversized/under-delivered frame wedges the whole server in blocking fread() until the declared bytes arrive or the pipe closes — no diagnostics/completion/hover served meanwhile. If the attacker actually streams the declared body, up to ~2GB is buffered in RAM per message (memory exhaustion on constrained hosts). No upper bound or sanity cap on the attacker-declared length exists. Not a memory-corruption bug (the allocation/fread/NUL-terminate are internally consistent), but it is the requested malloc(huge)/unbounded-length transport weakness.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[medium/low] REACHABILITY: Confirmed reachable with zero upstream guards. clat-lsp is a real built binary (Makefile:251 LSP_TARGET, built by `make test`). main() in src/lsp_main.c:52-92 does no validation; it sets stdin unbuffered and calls lsp_server_run(). The loop at src/lsp_server.c:3431-3433 invokes lsp_read_message(stdin) as the first code to touch raw stdin bytes. In src/lsp_protocol.c:16-25, the header is parsed via atoi(header+15) with the ONLY check being `if (content_length <= 0) return NULL;`, immediately followed by malloc((size_t)content_length+1) and a blocking fread of content_length bytes. I grepped the whole LSP subsystem for any cap/rlimit — none exists. The cited trigger (Content-Length: 2000000000 then stall) lands exactly on these lines.

ACCURACY: The mechanics are correct. No integer overflow (INT_MAX+1 fits in size_t even on 32-bit), so it is not memory corruption — the finder says so. fread blocks until the declared bytes arrive or EOF, wedging the single-threaded loop; a streamed body would buffer up to ~2GB. The behavioral description is faithful.

SKEPTICAL CAVEAT ON SEVERITY: This is a local LSP server spawned by the user's own editor over a private stdin/stdout pipe. The "untrusted editor" threat model is weak — whoever drives the JSON-RPC stream already controls the spawning process at equal privilege, and synchronous LSP framing inherently requires reading the full declared body before the next message (no multiplexing). So the only victim of a stall is the misbehaving client's own server (self-DoS within the user's trust domain), and the 2GB is virtual address space unless the attacker actually streams the data. This is a genuine but minor missing-sanity-cap robustness gap that production JSON-RPC stacks usually guard, not a privilege-crossing vulnerability. Verdict: real and reachable, but correctly scoped as low severity. Confidence is medium because while the code path and behavior are certain, the classification as a security bug (vs. inherent trusted-pipe transport behavior) is borderline. || [high/low] Re-derived the mechanism from the real code; it holds exactly as claimed with no misread bounds/types/ownership and no missed check. In src/lsp_protocol.c:8-35 (lsp_read_message): content_length = atoi(header+15) yields an int up to INT_MAX (~2.1B); the ONLY validation is `if (content_length <= 0) return NULL;` (line 19) — grep confirms no upper cap constant in lsp_protocol.c or include/lsp.h. Line 22 then does malloc((size_t)content_length + 1) (can attempt ~2GB), and line 25 does a single blocking fread of the full declared length. The cast to size_t happens before +1, so there is no integer overflow into a small alloc — the finder correctly characterizes this as a DoS/unbounded-length issue, NOT memory corruption. src/lsp_server.c:3431-3434 confirms the server is single-threaded: `for(;;){ cJSON *msg = lsp_read_message(stdin); ... }` processes one message per iteration, so an under-delivered frame wedges the whole server inside fread() until bytes arrive or the pipe closes. src/lsp_main.c:64 sets stdin to unbuffered/blocking (setvbuf _IONBF) with no timeout, corroborating the claimed runtime repro. Mechanism is real and internally consistent. Severity correctly stays low: the LSP server is a child process talking only to its parent editor over stdin/stdout pipes, so the party that can send a malicious Content-Length already fully controls the process (a malicious editor runs arbitrary code as the user). The impact is therefore self-inflicted/buggy-client robustness within an already-owned trust boundary, with no privilege boundary crossed and no memory corruption — a genuine but minor transport-hardening gap. || [high/low] Confirmed the cited code in src/lsp_protocol.c (lsp_read_message, lines 8-35). The only validation on the attacker-declared length is `if (content_length <= 0) return NULL;` (line 19). content_length is `atoi(header+15)` (line 16), which accepts any positive value up to INT_MAX (~2.1GB) with no overflow/range handling. The code then does `malloc((size_t)content_length + 1)` (line 22) and a blocking `fread(body, 1, content_length, in)` (line 25). There is NO upper bound or sanity cap anywhere in the read path — I searched the file and header for any cap and found none. The dispatch loop in src/lsp_server.c:3431-3433 (`for(;;) { msg = lsp_read_message(stdin); ... }`) is single-threaded, one message per iteration, so a blocking fread on an under-delivered body stalls the whole server. All factual claims hold; the only guards present (malloc-NULL -> return NULL -> loop breaks; short-read -> free+return NULL) prevent crashes/corruption but do not bound the allocation or the blocking wait. This is correctly NOT memory corruption, exactly as the finder states.

Refutation attempt: the server is stdio-only, and its sole input source is the editor that spawned it and already owns its lifecycle, so the wedge/2GB-buffer only denies service to the attacker's own instance (self-DoS), and blocking in fread is no worse than the server's inherent blocking in fgets waiting for the next header. That argument lowers the practical impact but does not refute the described behavior, and the project's own threat model (docs/security-audit-2026-06-27.md:493) explicitly treats lsp_server.c's editor JSON-RPC as untrusted input. Under that documented posture the missing length cap is a genuine low-severity hardening/robustness gap. Verdict: real, low severity, matching the finder's own honest classification.

confirmed.title: Unchecked realloc results dereferenced (NULL-write + leak) throughout analysis builders
confirmed.trigger: A very large untrusted document (many structs/enums/impl methods/braces/comments/tokens) that exhausts the heap so one of these reallocs returns NULL.
confirmed.consequence: On realloc failure the original pointer is overwritten with NULL (leak) and the immediately following indexed write dereferences NULL -> crash (DoS). Memory-pressure / OOM gated, hence low severity, but it is a genuine missing-NULL-check defect repeated at every growable-array append in the file.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] PATTERN CONFIRMED at every cited site (src/lsp_analysis.c:152-154, 211-212, 315-316, 330-331, 457-458, 477-479, 773-775, 814, 830, 842, 961): `ptr = realloc(ptr, n)` overwrites the original pointer with no NULL check, then the next statement does an indexed write (e.g. `doc->struct_defs[doc->struct_def_count - 1] = sdef;`). On realloc failure this leaks the old buffer and dereferences NULL+offset -> SIGSEGV.

REACHABILITY CONFIRMED from untrusted input. The defect is in `clat-lsp`, a real built target (Makefile: `LSP_TARGET = clat-lsp`, `make lsp`). Flow: `lsp_protocol.c:8-35` reads a JSON-RPC message — `Content-Length` is parsed with `atoi` into an `int` (cap only ~INT_MAX/~2GB) and the body read verbatim, with NO small size limit or document validation. `lsp_server.c` `didOpen` (523-525) and `didChange` (547-556) store the fully attacker-controlled document text and call `lsp_analyze_document(doc)` (lsp_analysis.c:1012). That function lexes/parses the untrusted text and runs `extract_symbols` (struct/enum/impl/symbol appends at 152/211/315/330), `extract_folding_ranges` (brace/comment folding appends at 457/477), and `extract_semantic_tokens` (comment/token appends at 773/814/830/842/961). Every realloc count is driven directly by attacker-controlled document content (number of declarations, braces, comments, tokens). No upstream guard, size cap, or count limit prevents arbitrarily large/dense documents. The threat model is realistic: a user opening a malicious .lat file (e.g. from a cloned repo) in an editor with the Lattice LSP feeds attacker content via didOpen.

GATING / SEVERITY: The NULL return is conditional on allocator failure (OOM, address-space/ulimit limits, or a pathologically large single allocation). The code PATH is unconditionally reachable, but the fault only fires when realloc actually returns NULL — which on a 64-bit overcommit system is uncommon, hence genuinely memory-pressure-gated. Consequence is a crash (SIGSEGV) of the LSP helper process (DoS; editor typically auto-restarts it) plus a small leak — no further memory corruption or RCE. The finder's `low` rating is correct. Verdict: real=true, severity low. (Note: this is the standard pervasive unchecked-realloc idiom; it is a genuine missing-NULL-check defect, not a false positive, and it is reachable.) || [high/low] Mechanism re-derived from the actual code and fully confirmed.
