confirmed.title: base64_decode is non-strict: unchecked trailing padding bits and mid-group '=' accepted (non-canonical / malleable encodings)
confirmed.trigger: base64_decode("Zh==") and base64_decode("Zg==") both return the single byte 0x66; base64_decode("Zm9=") and base64_decode("Zm8=") both return "fo". The decoder never verifies that the unused low bits of the last data symbol before padding are zero, so many distinct 4-char groups map to the same plaintext. It also accepts padding in the middle of a group (e.g. base64_decode("AB=C") returns 2 bytes) because the validity guard at line 670 only requires the 1st/2nd symbols to be non-pad and lets the 3rd be '=' while the 4th is data. Reachable directly from Lattice via the base64_decode() builtin (src/eval.c:5957, src/runtime.c:1430) on attacker-controlled input.
confirmed.consequence: Base64 is malleable/non-canonical: a given plaintext has many valid encodings, and structurally invalid strings decode successfully. In any downstream use that assumes a 1:1 encoding (token/signature/dedup/cache-key comparison on the encoded form, or round-trip equality checks), this permits forging alternate-but-equivalent inputs. No memory-safety impact.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Verified by reading the real code and reproducing the behavior. crypto_base64_decode (src/crypto_ops.c:632-690) is non-strict: (1) the guard at line 670 only forces positions 1-2 to be non-padding and tolerates '=' independently in positions 3 and 4, so a mid-group pad like "AB=C" passes and yields 2 bytes; (2) lines 677-684 zero-fill padded symbols and emit byte1 = (a<<2)|(b>>4), discarding the low 4 bits of symbol b with no zero-check, so "Zh=="/"Zg==" both decode to 0x66 and "Zm9="/"Zm8=" both to "fo". I built a standalone reproduction of the exact decode loop and got Zh==→66, Zg==→66, Zm9=→66 6f, Zm8=→66 6f, AB=C→00 02 — matching every trigger claim bit-for-bit. RFC 4648 §3.5 canonicality is not enforced. Reachability: confirmed, no upstream validator. base64_decode is a registered language builtin in all backends — tree-walker eval.c:5948-5961 and runtime native_base64_decode runtime.c:1423-1436 (registered at 4001 and 4371). Both only check argc==1 && type==VAL_STR, then forward args[0].as.str_val verbatim to crypto_base64_decode. Any Lattice program (e.g., an Anneal web handler) decoding attacker-controlled base64 hits this directly. Severity remains low: no memory-safety impact, and the malleability/non-canonical consequence is conditional on a downstream 1:1-encoding assumption; this lax behavior also matches many mainstream decoders. || [high/low] Mechanism re-derived from the actual code and empirically confirmed by compiling and running crypto_base64_decode (src/crypto_ops.c:632-690).

(1) Non-canonical/malleable acceptance: lines 676-684 substitute '=' with 0 (cv/dv) and emit bytes based only on whether c/d are padding, never verifying that the unused low bits of the last real symbol are zero. Confirmed: base64_decode("Zg==") and base64_decode("Zh==") both yield the single byte 0x66; "Zm8=" and "Zm9=" both yield "fo" (0x66 0x6f). Distinct 4-char groups collapse to identical plaintext, so a plaintext has many valid encodings.

(2) Mid-group padding accepted: the guard at line 670 (a<0 || b<0 || (c<0 && c!=-2) || (d<0 && d!=-2)) only forces symbols 1-2 non-pad and lets symbol 3 be '=' while symbol 4 is data. Confirmed: "AB=C" decodes to 2 bytes [0x00 0x02] instead of being rejected. (Note: padding in positions 1-2 is still correctly rejected — "A=BC" returns the invalid-character error because b<0 — but the claimed position-3 hole is real.)

Reachability confirmed: the builtin base64_decode dispatches to this function from both the tree-walk evaluator (src/eval.c:5957) and the runtime/RegVM native (src/runtime.c:1430) on attacker-controlled input. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, types, or any existing check — the only relevant check (line 670 + len%4 + valid-char) does not enforce canonical padding-bit zeroing or padding position, exactly as claimed.

No memory-safety impact (out buffer is sized (len/4)*3 and j never exceeds it). This is a correctness/crypto-strictness issue (non-strict RFC 4648 decoder). Real impact depends on downstream code assuming canonical 1:1 base64; on its own it is not exploitable, so "low" is the correct severity. || [medium/not-a-bug] The behavioral description is factually accurate. In src/crypto_ops.c:663-690 the decoder (a) never verifies that the unused low bits of the last data symbol before padding are zero, and (b) the validity guard at line 670 (`a < 0 || b < 0 || (c < 0 && c != -2) || (d < 0 && d != -2)`) only forces symbols 1/2 to be non-pad, so it accepts c=='=' with d being real data. I verified the arithmetic: "Zg==" and "Zh==" both yield out[0]=(n>>16)&0xFF=0x66 because 'h' (33) differs from 'g' (32) only in a bit that lands below bit 16 and is discarded; and "AB=C" produces 2 bytes (cv=0 because c==-2, dv=2; emits byte0=0 and byte3=2). So the decoder is genuinely lenient/non-canonical, exactly as claimed.

However, this does not rise to a security bug, and I can refute the "crypto-misuse" framing and the claimed consequence:

1. Lenient base64 decoding is explicitly RFC 4648 §3.5-permitted (rejecting non-canonical encodings is OPTIONAL). It is the default behavior of many mainstream libraries (e.g. Python base64.b64decode without validate=True, Java's java.util.Base64 tolerates trailing bits). It is a defensible design choice, not a correctness defect.

2. Base64 is a transport encoding, not a cryptographic primitive — it provides no integrity or authenticity. "Malleability" of base64 has no cryptographic consequence on its own; anyone who controls the plaintext already controls the encoding. Labeling this "crypto-misuse" is a mischaracterization.

3. No memory-safety impact (the finder concedes this). Output length is correctly bounded by max_out=(len/4)*3 and the len%4 check at line 650, so the extra output bytes never overflow.

4. The claimed consequence (forging alternate-but-equivalent inputs that defeat a downstream 1:1/round-trip comparison) is entirely conditional on a consumer that does not exist in this codebase. I grepped all callers: crypto_base64_decode is only invoked by the two user-facing builtin wrappers (src/eval.c:5957, src/runtime.c:1430). Nothing internal — no HMAC/token verification, signature comparison, dedup, or cache key — relies on canonical base64. The finder itself hedges with "In any downstream use that assumes a 1:1 encoding," i.e. a hypothetical.

5. Making the decoder strict would reject inputs that other RFC-conformant lenient encoders legitimately emit; that is a policy/hardening preference, not a bug fix.

Net: the code's leniency is real and accurately described, but in context it is RFC-permitted standard behavior with no security-sensitive consumer and no demonstrated consequence. Classifying it as a low-severity crypto-misuse bug is not warranted; this is at most an optional strictness-hardening nit, so I score it not-a-bug. Confidence is medium because a reviewer could reasonably defend flagging a lenient decoder living in a crypto module as a low hardening item.

confirmed.title: OpenSSL digest path ignores EVP return codes and disables its own error channel, silently returning an empty/incorrect hash on failure
confirmed.trigger: crypto_sha256/crypto_md5/crypto_sha512 (LATTICE_HAS_TLS, non-Schannel build) mark the error pointer (void)err and ignore the return values of EVP_DigestInit_ex/Update/Final. If the algorithm provider is unavailable (e.g. MD5 disabled under a FIPS OpenSSL provider, or low-memory), Init returns 0, hash_len stays 0, and hex_encode(hash,0) returns the empty string "". The callers (src/eval.c:5903/5921/5975, src/package.c:274) see cerr==NULL and treat "" as a valid digest.
confirmed.consequence: A digest failure is reported as success with an empty (constant, content-independent) hash string instead of an error. Anything relying on the digest for integrity (e.g. package.c content checksums, content-addressing, integrity comparisons) collapses to a single empty value for all inputs. The header's documented '*err on failure' contract is dead in the OpenSSL path. No memory-unsafety (hash_len is pre-initialized to 0, so no uninitialized read).
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Verified against the real source. src/crypto_ops.c:39-91 matches the snippet: crypto_sha256/md5/sha512 call EVP_DigestInit_ex/EVP_DigestUpdate/EVP_DigestFinal_ex and discard all three return codes; hash_len is initialized to 0; on a digest failure they fall through to hex_encode(hash, 0), which is malloc(1)+hash[0]='\0' = the empty string "" (not NULL). *err is set only in the !ctx branch (the `(void)err;` is otherwise contradicted there), so an algorithm/provider failure produces no error signal.

REACHABILITY (my mandate): the code path is default-active and reachable. Makefile:66 defines -DLATTICE_HAS_TLS without LATTICE_TLS_SCHANNEL on any Unix host with OpenSSL via pkg-config, so this exact block compiles and runs by default on Mac/Linux. It is reachable from untrusted input two ways: (1) user-script builtins sha256()/md5()/sha512() (src/eval.c:5896-5980) — any .lat source can call them; (2) pkg_compute_checksum (src/package.c:267-278) hashes fetched registry package content. No upstream guard prevents the trigger: callers only test `if (cerr)` (eval.c:5906/5924/5978) or `sum ? sum : strdup("")` (package.c:277), and since the OpenSSL path never sets *err on a digest failure, cerr stays NULL and "" is consumed as a valid hash. The header contract crypto_ops.h:7/11/20 ("Sets *err if unavailable") is genuinely dead in this path.

Caveat that bounds severity: the failure precondition is environmental, not input-controlled. The string content cannot itself make EVP Init/Update/Final return 0; the realistic triggers are MD5 under a FIPS OpenSSL provider (EVP_md5() NULL -> Init returns 0) or OOM. SHA-256/SHA-512 are FIPS-approved and essentially never fail outside OOM. So the silent empty-hash collapse is real and reachable but only manifests in an uncommon deployment, and there is no memory-unsafety (hash[] is a 64-byte stack buffer, hash_len pre-zeroed). This is a legitimate low-severity crypto-hygiene / contract-violation defect, not a false positive and not higher than low. || [high/low] Core mechanism HOLDS. In src/crypto_ops.c (the OpenSSL path, `#if defined(LATTICE_HAS_TLS) && !defined(LATTICE_TLS_SCHANNEL)`), crypto_sha256 (lines 39-55), crypto_md5 (57-73), and crypto_sha512 (75-91) all call EVP_DigestInit_ex / EVP_DigestUpdate / EVP_DigestFinal_ex WITHOUT checking any of their return codes (lines 49-51, 67-69, 85-87). `hash_len` is pre-initialized to 0 (lines 47/65/83). If the digest provider is unavailable (e.g. MD5 under a FIPS OpenSSL provider, or an allocation/hardware failure), EVP_DigestInit_ex returns 0, the digest is never computed, hash_len stays 0, and `hex_encode(hash, 0)` (lines 19-25) does `malloc(0*2+1)`, runs zero loop iterations, sets `hex[0]='\0'` and returns the empty string "" — with NO error set and no uninitialized read of `hash` (the finder's no-memory-unsafety claim is correct since len=0 means zero bytes are read).

Caller behavior confirmed: at src/eval.c:5902-5907 (sha256), 5920-5925 (md5), 5974-5979 (sha512) the pattern is `if (cerr) return eval_err(cerr); return eval_ok(value_string_owned(result));` — so a NULL cerr with result=="" is returned as a successful, content-independent constant digest. src/package.c:267-278 (pkg_compute_checksum, used for lattice.lock integrity) likewise returns the "" sum as success. The header (include/crypto_ops.h:7,10,19) documents "Sets *err if unavailable", a contract that is dead for the actual digest-computation failures.

ONE INACCURACY in the finding's phrasing: it claims the function "disables its own error channel" via `(void)err`. That is overstated — `(void)err;` (lines 40/58/76) is a redundant unused-suppression cast that has NO effect, because `*err` IS in fact still written in the `if (!ctx)` branch (lines 43/61/79) for the EVP_MD_CTX_new() allocation-failure path. So the error channel is partially live (ctx alloc), just not wired to the Init/Update/Final return codes. This mischaracterization does not invalidate the substantive bug: the EVP operation return codes are genuinely ignored and a failure silently yields an empty/incorrect hash reported as success.

Severity: low is appropriate. The trigger requires an atypical environment (FIPS/legacy-provider disablement of the algorithm, or OOM/hardware failure mid-digest); in normal builds SHA-256/512/MD5 always succeed. But the consequence (silent constant "" digest collapsing integrity/content-addressing) is real, and ignoring EVP return codes is a textbook crypto-misuse defensive-coding defect. Confidence high because the missing return-code checks are unambiguous in the source. || [high/low] Verified against the real code. In src/crypto_ops.c:39-91 (LATTICE_HAS_TLS, non-Schannel), crypto_sha256/md5/sha512 call EVP_DigestInit_ex (lines 49/67/85), EVP_DigestUpdate (50/68/86) and EVP_DigestFinal_ex (51/69/87) WITHOUT checking any return value. hash_len is pre-initialized to 0 (47/65/83), so if Init/Update/Final fail (return 0) hash_len stays 0 and hex_encode(hash,0) at line 19-25 does malloc(1), writes hex[0]='\0', and returns the empty string "". The function then returns "" with *err untouched. The header (include/crypto_ops.h:7,10,19) documents "Sets *err if unavailable", so this silently violates the contract. Callers in src/eval.c (sha256 5903-08, md5 5921-26, sha512 5975-80) do `if (cerr) return eval_err(cerr); return eval_ok(value_string_owned(result))` — with cerr==NULL they accept "" as a valid digest; src/package.c:274 likewise accepts a non-NULL "" sum. The core claim — EVP return codes ignored, silent empty/incorrect hash on failure — is objectively true and there is NO guard, length check, or post-Final validation anywhere in the path, so I cannot refute it. Caveats lowering the impact (and partially correcting the finder): (1) the "(void)err disables its own error channel" framing is inaccurate — *err IS still used at line 43 for the EVP_MD_CTX_new() NULL case, so the channel is only dead for the three digest steps; (2) the dramatic "package.c content-addressing collapses for all inputs" consequence is essentially unreachable because package.c uses sha256 and EVP_sha256() is always present (even under FIPS), so its Init does not realistically fail; (3) the only realistic failure trigger is crypto_md5 under a strict FIPS provider (MD5 restricted) or hard OOM — narrow, exotic conditions. Net: a genuine but low-severity error-handling/crypto-robustness defect (ignored EVP return codes -> silent empty hash + unmet *err contract), with no memory unsafety. "low" is the correct severity.

confirmed.title: Per-function memory leak of duplicated param name in ext_load
confirmed.trigger: Every successful require_ext() of an uncached extension, once per registered function. value_closure() (src/value.c:293-294) does lat_strdup() of each param name (it copies, it does not take ownership), so the strdup("args") allocation in pname is never reachable again: only the param_names array is freed, not pname.
confirmed.consequence: Small unconditional heap leak (one strdup of "args" per exported extension function per load). Bounded and minor, but a genuine leak. The malloc-failure early-return at line 339 also leaks the just-allocated pname, the partially-built map, and remaining ctx.reg entries, though that path is OOM-only.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Confirmed by reading the actual code. src/ext.c:337-346 allocates pname = strdup("args") (libc malloc), stores it in param_names[0], and passes the array to value_closure(). value_closure() (src/value.c:293-294) makes its OWN copies: it lat_alloc's a fresh param_names array and lat_strdup()'s each name into it — it does not take ownership of the caller's strings. The caller then does free(param_names), freeing only the 1-element array, never pname. Therefore the strdup("args") block is leaked once per registered extension function. value_free (value.c:1595-1600) only frees the closure's own arena-routed copies, never the caller's pname, so nothing reclaims it.

Reachability is confirmed: ext_load is invoked from the user-callable builtin require_ext(name), dispatched in the tree-walker at src/eval.c:3317 and via native_require_ext at src/runtime.c:3017 / regvm.c:4184. require_ext is a documented builtin (see completion.c keyword list), so a plain .lat source file run from the CLI can call it. The only upstream validators in ext_load (lines 245-253) reject empty names and names containing '/' or '\\' — neither guards the closure-building loop. Reaching the loop additionally requires a real extension .dylib/.so present in one of the search paths (./extensions/, ~/.lattice/ext/, $LATTICE_EXT_PATH) exposing lat_ext_init and registering >=1 function, which is exactly the normal use of the extension feature. So the trigger is reachable from real input.

Magnitude: the tree-walker caches results in ev->loaded_extensions, so the leak fires once per (distinct extension x registered function) rather than per call — bounded and small (~5 bytes each). The malloc-failure early return at line 339 is OOM-only and additionally leaks pname/partial map, a strictly minor sub-case. Genuine leak, low severity is accurate; not a security or DoS concern under normal usage. || [high/low] Verified against the real code, not the finder's snippet. In src/ext.c:335-347, the per-function loop does: pname = strdup("args") (alloc A), param_names = malloc(sizeof(char*)) (alloc B), param_names[0] = pname, then value_closure(param_names, 1, ...), then free(param_names). value_closure (src/value.c:290-301) does NOT take ownership of the passed array or strings: line 293 allocates its own array via lat_alloc, and line 294 copies each name with lat_strdup. lat_strdup (src/value.c:186-192) in the normal (non-arena) path does lat_alloc + memcpy, producing an independent copy. Therefore the closure stores its own "args" string; the caller's pname (alloc A) is never referenced again. The subsequent free(param_names) frees only the array (B), leaving pname (A) leaked. This is a genuine unconditional heap leak of one strdup("args") per registered extension function per successful uncached require_ext()/ext_load. The OOM early-return at line 339 (if (!param_names) return value_unit();) is also confirmed to leak pname plus the partially-built map and remaining ctx.reg entries, but that path is OOM-only. The leak is real, bounded, and minor — extensions typically load once and remain for process lifetime — so 'low' severity is correct. No misread of ownership, bounds, or existing checks by the finder. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading the actual code. In src/ext.c:337-346, `char *pname = strdup("args")` allocates a plain libc heap block. It is stored into `param_names[0]`, passed to `value_closure(param_names, 1, ...)`, then only `free(param_names)` (the array) is called — `pname` itself is never freed. value_closure (src/value.c:290-301) does NOT take ownership: lines 293-294 allocate a fresh array and `lat_strdup` each name, and lat_strdup (src/value.c:186-192) is a true copy (lat_alloc + memcpy). Therefore the closure stores its own copy of "args"; the original `pname` is unreferenced after `free(param_names)`. No caching, value_free, or other path reaches `pname`. `strdup` in ext.c is plain libc (string.h, no #define override at line 337/60). Net: a genuine unconditional leak of one strdup("args") per registered extension function per uncached ext_load. It is bounded and tiny (5 bytes each, finite functions, few loads per process), so severity "low" is appropriate. The malloc-failure early-return at line 339 additionally leaks pname plus the partially built map and remaining ctx.reg entries, but that is OOM-only. I attempted to refute via ownership-transfer or caching but value_closure's copy semantics make refutation impossible.
